judgments.comparative_verdict: Both correctly block on the tenant-isolation leak and both miss the exports.py broken caller, but Report 1 edges ahead by hedging the raw-SQL status interpolation as non-exploitable-as-written whereas Report 2 overstates it as a critical SQL-injection vulnerability.

report_1.evidence: Location: backend/app/appointments.py:10-18 (search_appointments function) ... The tenant_id parameter received by the function is never used in the SQL WHERE clause ... Comparison with main branch's build_appointment_query ... shows the established pattern requires Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id

report_2.evidence: File and location: backend/app/appointments.py, lines 10-20 (entire search_appointments function) ... The function receives tenant_id as a parameter but never uses it in the WHERE clause ... Any user can search and retrieve appointments from ALL tenants in the database

report_1.evidence: Although the enum currently limits status to {booked, cancelled, completed}, if the enum is modified or the code is refactored, malicious status values could inject SQL.

report_2.evidence: DEFECT A: SQL Injection Vulnerability ... this violates secure SQL practices and creates a SQL injection vector. An attacker who can manipulate the enum or bypass type checking could inject arbitrary SQL to extract all database data
